summaries:
This conversation covers the onboarding and pipeline design for a new customer, Acme Corp, with Jane Smith serving as the primary contact at jane.smith@acmecorp.io in her role as Director of Data Engineering. Procurement closed yesterday, and the team is now working through technical implementation details under the tracking thread acme-pipeline-design, which includes subtasks for schema mapping, DAG authoring, and monitoring.

The team decided to use AWS MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) instead of self-hosted Argo Workflows, primarily because MWAA allows Acme to inherit SOC 2 compliance through AWS's attestation, satisfying their SOC 2 requirement without requiring separate attestation. The managed service also reduces operational burden. Infrastructure setup includes the S3 bucket s3://acme-intake-prod-492372 with KMS encryption, versioning enabled, and a lifecycle policy transitioning data to Glacier at 90 days. Acme's data volume is approximately 40GB per day with peaks at 80GB, consisting mostly of transactional CSVs and some Avro files, totaling around 12TB per year at steady state.

The service level agreement specifies 6-hour data freshness and 99.5% uptime under the standard tier without premium add-ons. Compliance requirements include handling PII data such as emails and phone numbers, which requires tokenization at ingest, and achieving SOC 2 Type II attestation by Q3. Schema drift is managed through Great Expectations validations on every DAG run, with schema changes triggering Slack alerts and pausing downstream DAGs. The backfill strategy uses DAG-level catchup=False, with manual triggering for explicit date ranges; Acme's historical data involved a 30-day backfill completed over a weekend.

Source-side changes are handled through a webhook at /internal/acme/source-change that pauses affected DAGs and requires human acknowledgment to resume. A separate MWAA small environment in the dev account serves as the test environment, mirroring the production DAG layout with a synthetic data generator running nightly. Cost management includes weekly internal reviews in the #data-pipelines channel with costs tagged customer:acme for attribution. Cross-team dependencies involve the platform team owning the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns Acme-specific DAGs, with breaking changes reviewed via pull requests. Two open low-priority tickets address schema drift alert tuning due to high false-positive rates and Macie cost optimization to reduce scanning frequency from hourly to twice daily. The runbook is located at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md and covers on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift response, and cost spike response, though these runbooks remain internal while Acme maintains a customer-facing status page at status.acme.example.com.

The team has transitioned from design to build phase for the Acme pipeline project, renaming the thread from `acme-pipeline-design` to `acme-pipeline-build`. The pipeline architecture consists of three separate DAGs—one each for transactions, customers, and products data sources—to ensure failure isolation so that issues with one source don't block the others. The DAG code lives in dags/acme/main.py and imports from a shared DAG factory utility.

Infrastructure and security are configured around AWS account 492372116065. Encryption uses the KMS key arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:492372116065:key/abc-def-ghi-jkl for both S3 buckets and MWAA secrets. The MWAA execution role arn:aws:iam::492372116065:role/mwaa-acme-execution has permissions for S3 read/write on the intake bucket, Secrets Manager read on /acme/*, and KMS decrypt on the project key. For PII handling, the pipeline uses AWS Macie for detection in the staging zone, followed by Format-Preserving Encryption via a custom Python function using the cryptography library, with tokenization keys stored in /acme/tokenization.

The rollout schedule has the transactions DAG going live tomorrow morning on an hourly schedule, with the customers DAG following the day after on a daily schedule. Schema drift is managed through Great Expectations validations on every DAG run, with schema changes triggering Slack alerts and pausing downstream DAGs. The backfill strategy uses catchup=False at the DAG level, with manual triggering for explicit date ranges—the initial Acme historical data backfill covered 30 days and was completed over a weekend. Source-side changes are handled via a webhook to /internal/acme/source-change, which pauses affected DAGs and requires human acknowledgment to resume.

The testing and operational setup includes a separate small MWAA environment in the dev account that mirrors the production DAG layout, with a synthetic data generator running nightly. Cost management uses `customer:acme` tags for clean attribution, with weekly internal reviews by the team in #data-pipelines. Cross-team dependencies involve the platform team owning the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns the Acme-specific DAGs, with breaking changes reviewed via PR. Two open tickets exist as low-priority items: tuning schema drift alerts due to high false-positive rates, and optimizing Macie costs by reducing runs from hourly to twice daily. The operational runbook is located at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md and includes on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift response, and cost spike response, while Acme maintains a customer-facing status page at status.acme.example.com.

The conversation begins with the Acme pipeline now operational and shifts focus to monitoring and cost optimization work. The original thread acme-pipeline-build is split into two new threads: acme-monitoring and acme-cost-optimization to handle these distinct workstreams.

The monitoring strategy combines Datadog for runtime metrics such as DAG duration and task success or failure rates, with CloudWatch handling AWS infrastructure monitoring for S3, MWAA, and Lambda services. Alerts are routed through a PagerDuty integration identified as acme-pipeline-prod. Current monthly costs run approximately $3400, broken down as $2100 for compute, $700 for storage, $400 for transfer, and $200 for Macie. The cost optimization target aims to reduce spending to under $2500 per month, primarily by switching to a smaller MWAA environment (mw1.small) after 60 days of steady-state validation, which should save roughly $900 monthly.

The team has experienced one SLA breach to date. On Friday, the source system held a batch for four hours, causing the pipeline to exceed its six-hour freshness SLA. Acme acknowledged this was a source-side issue stemming from an uncommunicated maintenance window, and no penalty was applied. This incident has been documented in the incident log with the root cause clearly identified.

Schema drift is managed through Great Expectations validations that run on every DAG execution. When schema changes are detected, the system flags a Slack alert and pauses downstream DAGs. The backfill strategy uses DAG-level catchup=False, with backfills triggered manually using explicit date ranges. The initial Acme historical data backfill covered 30 days and was completed over a weekend. Source-side changes are communicated via a webhook to /internal/acme/source-change, which pauses affected DAGs and requires human acknowledgment before resuming operations.

A separate test environment exists as a MWAA small environment in the dev account, mirroring the production DAG layout with a synthetic data generator running nightly. Cost reporting occurs weekly within the team's #data-pipelines channel, with all costs tagged as customer:acme for clean attribution. Cross-team dependencies involve the platform team owning the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns the Acme-specific DAGs, with the platform team reviewing any breaking changes via pull requests.

Two open tickets remain as low priority items: tuning schema drift alerts to reduce false-positive rates, and optimizing Macie costs by reducing its run frequency from hourly to twice daily. The runbook is located at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md and includes on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift responses, and cost spike responses. These runbooks are strictly internal, while Acme maintains a customer-facing status page at status.acme.example.com. The conversation shows some repetition in the latter portion as the same operational questions are asked and answered multiple times.

This conversation slice covers an operational review of the Acme data pipeline infrastructure, with the user conducting a comprehensive status check and exploring various operational aspects. The system is currently stable with all three DAGs running steadily, monitored through Datadog, CloudWatch, and PagerDuty. Cost optimization efforts are underway, with the team 30 days out from switching to an MWAA small environment. SOC 2 compliance has been successfully completed for the Acme inheritance scope, with evidence collection finished and auditor approval received.

A new decision was made during this conversation to implement an automated weekly cost dashboard that will be sent to Jane Smith via SES. Two active threads remain open: acme-monitoring and acme-cost-optimization, while the original build thread has been completed. The team has implemented robust data quality controls using Great Expectations validations on every DAG run, which detect schema changes and trigger Slack alerts while pausing downstream DAGs to prevent data issues.

The backfill strategy is conservative, with DAG-level catchup set to False and all backfills triggered manually with explicit date ranges. The initial Acme historical data backfill covered 30 days and was executed over a weekend. Source-side changes are managed through a webhook at /internal/acme/source-change, which pauses affected DAGs and requires human acknowledgment before resuming operations. A separate MWAA small environment exists in the dev account for testing, mirroring the production DAG layout and running a synthetic data generator nightly.

Cost management is handled through weekly internal reviews in the #data-pipelines Slack channel, with all costs tagged as customer:acme for clean attribution. Cross-team coordination exists with the platform team, who owns the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns the Acme-specific DAGs, with breaking changes reviewed via pull requests. Two low-priority tickets are currently open: tuning schema drift alerts to reduce false-positive rates and optimizing Macie to run twice daily instead of hourly. Operational documentation is maintained at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md, covering on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift responses, and cost spike responses. These runbooks are internal-only, while Acme has access to a customer-facing status page at status.acme.example.com.

metas.summary:
This session covered the initial design and operational setup for a new customer data pipeline for Acme Corp. The primary contact is Jane Smith, Director of Data Engineering, with procurement closed yesterday. The core architectural decision was to use AWS MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) rather than self-hosted Argo Workflows, driven primarily by SOC 2 compliance requirements and reduced operational burden.

Infrastructure setup included creating the S3 intake bucket (s3://acme-intake-prod-492372) with KMS encryption, versioning, and a 90-day lifecycle policy to Glacier. The pipeline handles approximately 40GB/day of data (peak 80GB), primarily transactional CSVs and some Avro, with an expected steady-state volume of 12TB/year. The SLA is 6-hour data freshness with 99.5% uptime on the standard tier.

Compliance and data handling requirements include PII tokenization at ingest (emails and phone numbers present) and a SOC 2 Type II attestation needed by Q3. Schema drift is managed via Great Expectations validations on every DAG run, with schema changes triggering Slack alerts and pausing downstream DAGs. Source-side changes are handled through a webhook notification system at /internal/acme/source-change, requiring human acknowledgment before resuming affected DAGs.

The operational model includes a separate MWAA small environment in the dev account for testing, with a synthetic data generator running nightly. Backfills are manual with explicit date ranges (catchup=False), with the initial 30-day historical backfill completed over a weekend. Cost reporting happens weekly in #data-pipelines with customer:acme tagging for attribution. Cross-team dependencies exist with the platform team owning the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns Acme-specific DAGs. Two low-priority tickets are open: tuning schema drift alert false-positive rates and optimizing Macie scan frequency from hourly to twice-daily. The runbook is located at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md and covers on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift response, and cost spike response.

metas.summary:
This session transitioned from design to implementation of the Acme Corp data pipeline. The primary thread was renamed from `acme-pipeline-design` to `acme-pipeline-build` to reflect the shift to active construction. The core architectural decision was to split the pipeline into three separate DAGs—one each for transactions, customers, and products—to provide failure isolation so that issues with one data source don't block the others.

The DAG code lives at dags/acme/main.py and imports from utils.dag_factory. The transactions DAG goes live tomorrow morning on an hourly schedule, with the customers DAG following the day after on a daily schedule. PII tokenization uses AWS Macie for detection in the staging zone, followed by Format-Preserving Encryption via a Python custom function using the cryptography library. Tokenization keys are stored in AWS Secrets Manager at /acme/tokenization.

Infrastructure details were confirmed: the KMS key (arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:492372116065:key/abc-def-ghi-jkl) is shared between S3 bucket encryption and MWAA secrets. The MWAA execution role (arn:aws:iam::492372116065:role/mwaa-acme-execution) has S3 read/write on the intake bucket, Secrets Manager read on /acme/*, and KMS decrypt permissions on the project key. The operational model, schema drift handling, backfill strategy, source change notifications, test environment, cost reporting, cross-team dependencies, and runbook location all remain as previously established. The two low-priority optimization tickets (schema drift alert tuning and Macie scan frequency reduction) are still open.

metas.summary:
This session shifted focus from pipeline implementation to operational monitoring and cost optimization for the Acme Corp data pipeline. The primary thread was split from `acme-pipeline-build` into two new active threads: `acme-monitoring` for observability setup and `acme-cost-optimization` for reducing monthly spend. The pipelines are now running in production.

The monitoring strategy uses Datadog for runtime metrics (DAG duration, task success/failure rates) and CloudWatch for AWS infrastructure metrics (S3, MWAA, Lambda). Alerts route through PagerDuty integration `acme-pipeline-prod`. Current monthly costs are approximately $3400 (compute $2100, storage $700, transfer $400, Macie $200), with a target to reduce to under $2500/month. The primary cost optimization plan is to switch to a MWAA small environment (mw1.small) after 60 days of steady-state validation, which should save approximately $900/month.

One SLA breach occurred on Friday when the source system held a 4-hour batch that exceeded the 6-hour freshness SLA. The root cause was a source-side maintenance window that was not communicated in advance. Acme acknowledged the source-side cause and no penalty was applied. This incident was documented in the incident log. The two low-priority optimization tickets (schema drift alert tuning and Macie scan frequency reduction) remain open and active.

metas.summary:
This session was a status check and operational review of the Acme Corp production pipeline. All three DAGs (transactions, customers, products) are running steadily with monitoring fully operational via Datadog, CloudWatch, and PagerDuty. The SOC 2 evidence collection for the Acme inheritance scope is complete and has been reviewed by the auditor with no issues. Cost optimization remains in progress, with the MWAA small environment switchover planned in approximately 30 days.

A new decision was made to implement an automated weekly cost dashboard for Acme, delivered via AWS SES to Jane Smith. This adds customer-facing cost transparency to the existing internal weekly cost review process in #data-pipelines. The conversation included multiple reviews of operational procedures (schema drift handling, backfill strategy, source change notifications, test environment setup, cross-team dependencies, and runbook location), confirming that all established processes remain in place and unchanged from previous sessions.

The two low-priority optimization tickets (schema drift alert tuning and Macie scan frequency reduction) remain open and active. The original pipeline build thread is now completed, with monitoring and cost optimization continuing as the primary active work streams.

conv-data-pipeline.expectedAnswer: Managed service reduces ops burden + SOC 2 inheritance via AWS

conv-data-pipeline.expectedAnswer: Failure isolation — one source failing shouldn't block others

kasett.answer: Switch MWAA environment from current size to mw1.small in approximately 30 days, reducing compute costs from the current $3400/mo to the target $2500/mo.
